q: What are the main stages of a typical CI/CD pipeline?
a: A common pipeline runs lint/test, then build, then package into an image or artifact, then deploy to an environment. Each stage gates the next: tests must pass before you build, and the build must succeed before you deploy. Later stages often add security scanning, integration tests, and promotion across dev/staging/prod. The goal is to catch failures as early and cheaply as possible (shift left).
fs: I order stages by cost-to-fix: fast unit tests first, expensive integration/deploy last, so we fail cheap.
fj: You write code, then push to prod.

q: What are the three core components of a GitHub Actions workflow?
a: A workflow is a YAML file in .github/workflows triggered by events (on:). It contains jobs, and each job runs on a runner and is made up of steps. Steps either run shell commands (run:) or invoke reusable actions (uses:). Jobs run in parallel by default unless you create dependencies with needs:.
fs: Events trigger workflows; jobs run on runners and steps are either run: shell or uses: actions, sequenced with needs:.
fj: It's just a YAML file that runs my scripts somehow.

q: How do you make one GitHub Actions job wait for another?
a: Use the needs: keyword to declare a dependency on another job. The dependent job won't start until all jobs it needs have completed successfully. This turns parallel jobs into a sequenced graph, for example test -> build -> deploy. If a needed job fails, downstream jobs are skipped unless you override with if: always() or a condition.
fs: needs: builds the job DAG; I gate deploy on test+build and use if: to control skip-on-failure behavior.
fj: All jobs run top to bottom in the order I wrote them.

q: How does dependency caching work in GitHub Actions, and why use restore-keys?
a: actions/cache saves a directory (like ~/.npm) under a key, usually a hash of the lockfile. On a later run, an exact key match restores the cache (a cache hit); if the lockfile changed, the key misses. restore-keys provides ordered prefix fallbacks so you still restore a recent partial cache instead of starting cold. The cache-hit output is true only on an exact primary-key match.
fs: I key the cache on hashFiles of the lockfile and add an OS-prefix restore-key so a lockfile bump warm-starts from the last cache.
fj: Caching just makes builds faster, I don't set a key.

q: Why is caching not a substitute for a clean, reproducible install?
a: A cache is a performance optimization, not a source of truth, so a poisoned or stale cache must never change the result. You still run a deterministic install like npm ci against the lockfile; the cache only skips re-downloading already-resolved packages. Keying on the lockfile hash guarantees a dependency change invalidates the cache. If correctness ever depends on the cache existing, you have a bug.
fs: Cache is keyed on the lockfile and only speeds re-downloads; npm ci still resolves from the lock so a poisoned cache can't change output.
fj: I cache node_modules and skip install to save time.

q: How should secrets be handled in a CI/CD pipeline?
a: Store secrets in the platform's encrypted secret store (GitHub Actions secrets, environment secrets, or an external vault), never in the repo. Inject them as masked environment variables at runtime and scope them to the minimum jobs/environments that need them. Prefer short-lived, least-privilege credentials and rotate long-lived ones. Be aware secrets are not passed to workflows triggered by forks, and masked values can still leak if you echo them to logs.
fs: Secrets live in an encrypted store, are scoped to environments, masked in logs, and least-privilege; I avoid passing them to fork PRs.
fj: I put the API key in the YAML so the job can use it.

q: Why is OIDC preferred over storing long-lived cloud credentials as secrets?
a: With OIDC, GitHub Actions requests a short-lived token from the cloud provider, which trusts GitHub's identity provider and issues credentials scoped to a specific repo/branch/environment. This eliminates long-lived access keys stored as secrets, so there is nothing to leak or rotate. Tokens expire after the job and carry only the permissions the cloud role grants. It is the current best practice for cloud auth from pipelines.
fs: OIDC swaps static keys for a federated short-lived token scoped by subject claims, so there's no secret to rotate or exfiltrate.
fj: I store the AWS access key and secret as repo secrets, that's secure enough.

q: What is an environment approval gate and how do you configure one in GitHub Actions?
a: An environment (e.g. production) can have protection rules, including required reviewers who must manually approve before any job targeting that environment runs. Up to six users or teams can be reviewers, and only one needs to approve; you can also forbid self-review. Other rules include wait timers and branch restrictions. The job references the environment with environment: and pauses, surfacing an approval prompt in the run.
fs: I gate prod with an environment plus required reviewers and enable prevent-self-review so the deployer can't approve their own push.
fj: I just add a comment saying please approve before merging.

q: What is a matrix build and when is it useful?
a: A matrix runs the same job across combinations of variables, such as multiple OSes and language versions, expanding automatically into one job per combination. It is ideal for testing a library across Node 18/20/22 on Linux/Windows/macOS in parallel. include adds extra one-off combinations and exclude removes invalid ones. This gives broad compatibility coverage without duplicating job definitions.
fs: Matrix fans one job spec across version x OS combos; I use include for edge configs and exclude to drop incompatible pairs.
fj: I copy-paste the test job once per Node version.

q: What do fail-fast and max-parallel control in a matrix strategy?
a: fail-fast (default true) cancels all other in-flight matrix jobs as soon as one fails, saving runner minutes when you want a quick red signal. Setting it false lets every combination finish so you see the full failure matrix, useful for flaky cross-version debugging. max-parallel caps how many matrix jobs run concurrently, which throttles load on limited or self-hosted runners. Together they trade speed-of-signal against completeness and resource use.
fs: I keep fail-fast on for PRs to fail cheap, flip it off on nightly cross-version runs, and cap max-parallel for self-hosted runners.
fj: fail-fast just retries the failed job.

q: What does 'build once, deploy many' mean and why does it matter?
a: You produce a single immutable artifact (e.g. a container image) once in CI, then promote that exact artifact through dev, staging, and prod rather than rebuilding per environment. Rebuilding risks drift from changed dependencies, tool versions, or transient network state, so what you tested may not be what you ship. Promoting the same digest guarantees the tested artifact is the deployed one. Environment differences are injected via config, not by rebuilding.
fs: One immutable build flows through environments; config is injected at deploy so the digest tested equals the digest shipped.
fj: I just run the build step again in the prod pipeline.

q: What is artifact promotion and how is it usually implemented?
a: Promotion advances a single already-built artifact from one environment or stage to the next after it passes gates, without rebuilding. Tag-based promotion keeps the image in one registry and adds tags (e.g. :staging then :prod), while registry-based promotion copies it to a higher-trust registry. You should pin to the immutable digest, not a mutable tag, since tags can be overwritten. Each promotion is logged with actor, timestamp, and digest for audit.
fs: I promote by referencing the image digest, gate each hop on scans/tests, and keep an audit trail of who promoted what.
fj: Promotion means rebuilding the app with the prod config.

q: Why pin deployments to an image digest instead of a tag like :latest?
a: Tags are mutable pointers: :latest or :prod can be re-pushed to point at a different image, so two deploys of the same tag may ship different code. A digest (sha256:...) is content-addressed and immutable, uniquely identifying one exact image. Pinning to the digest makes deploys reproducible, auditable, and rollback-safe. It also defends against a supply-chain swap of a tag you trusted.
fs: I deploy app@sha256:... because tags are mutable pointers; the digest is the only reproducible, tamper-evident reference.
fj: We always deploy :latest so we get the newest build.

q: Explain blue-green deployment and its rollback behavior.
a: Blue-green runs two identical production environments: blue serves live traffic while green gets the new version. After validating green, you flip the load balancer (or DNS) to send all traffic to it instantly. Rollback is just flipping back to blue, which is near-instant since the old environment is still warm. The cost is running double infrastructure during the cutover.
fs: Blue-green swaps all traffic via the LB to a parallel stack, so rollback is a one-switch flip back to the warm blue env.
fj: Blue-green means slowly sending more users to the new version.

q: Explain canary deployment and how it differs from blue-green.
a: Canary routes a small percentage of traffic (e.g. 1% -> 5% -> 25% -> 100%) to the new version while watching error rates and latency, expanding only if metrics stay healthy. Unlike blue-green's instant all-or-nothing switch, canary exposes risk gradually so only a slice of users hit a bad release. It doesn't require a full duplicate environment, but it needs traffic-splitting and good observability. Rollback means shifting that slice back to the stable version.
fs: Canary shifts traffic in metric-gated increments, limiting blast radius; blue-green is an instant 100% cutover with a full duplicate stack.
fj: Canary and blue-green are basically the same thing.

q: What makes a deployment rollback fast and safe?
a: Fast rollback comes from keeping the previous known-good immutable artifact available so you can re-point traffic or redeploy it without rebuilding. Stateless app changes roll back cleanly; the hard part is database migrations, which should be backward-compatible (expand/contract) so old and new code both work during the window. Automated health checks should trigger or at least prompt rollback. Avoid destructive, irreversible schema changes in the same release as code.
fs: I keep the prior artifact warm and use expand/contract migrations so rollback is a re-point, not a rebuild or a data-loss event.
fj: Rollback means git revert and redeploy from scratch.

q: Why are forward-compatible database migrations important for zero-downtime deploys?
a: During a rolling or canary deploy, old and new code run simultaneously, so the schema must satisfy both. The expand/contract pattern splits a change: first expand (add the new nullable column/table) before code uses it, then migrate data, then contract (drop the old column) only after all code stops using it. This avoids the new code referencing a column the old code can't tolerate, or vice versa. It also makes rollback safe because the prior version still runs against the expanded schema.
fs: I use expand/contract: add-before-use, drop-after-unused, so mixed old/new versions and rollback both work against the schema.
fj: I just ALTER the table and deploy the new code together.

q: How do you build a container image inside a GitHub Actions pipeline?
a: Typically you check out the code, set up Buildx, authenticate to a registry (ideally via OIDC), then build and push tagged with the commit SHA. Layer caching (cache-from/cache-to or a registry cache) speeds rebuilds. The pushed image becomes the immutable artifact later stages promote and deploy. Tagging with the immutable SHA, not just :latest, keeps the artifact traceable to a commit.
fs: I build with Buildx, tag by commit SHA, push via OIDC auth, and use registry-backed layer cache for fast rebuilds.
fj: I run docker build and docker push :latest from my laptop.

q: How do you pass data between jobs in a GitHub Actions pipeline?
a: Jobs run on separate runners with no shared filesystem, so you can't rely on files persisting. Use job outputs (set via $GITHUB_OUTPUT) for small values like a version or image tag, declared with outputs: and read through needs.<job>.outputs. For larger build products, use actions/upload-artifact in one job and download-artifact in another. Caches are for dependencies, not for handing off build outputs between dependent jobs.
fs: Small values flow via job outputs and needs.<job>.outputs; build products move via upload/download-artifact, since runners share no disk.
fj: I just write a file in one job and read it in the next.

q: What is concurrency control in GitHub Actions and why use it for deploys?
a: The concurrency: key groups runs under a named key and ensures only one runs at a time per group, optionally cancelling in-progress runs. For deploys you set a group like deploy-prod with cancel-in-progress false so two deploys never race to the same environment, queuing instead. For PR CI you often set cancel-in-progress true so a new push cancels the stale run. This prevents conflicting concurrent deployments and wasted runner time.
fs: I scope a concurrency group per environment with cancel-in-progress false so prod deploys serialize instead of racing.
fj: GitHub already prevents two workflows running at once, so I don't worry about it.

q: Why run security/dependency scanning as a pipeline stage, and how do you avoid blocking everything?
a: Adding SAST, dependency, and image-vulnerability scans (e.g. CodeQL, Trivy) to the pipeline shifts security left so issues are caught before deploy. To avoid drowning teams, you gate the build only on high/critical severity and on newly introduced findings, not the entire pre-existing backlog. Results feed dashboards and PR annotations, and you set SLAs to burn down lower-severity items. The scan acts as a gate before promotion to higher environments.
fs: I fail the build only on new high/critical findings and track the rest with SLAs, so scanning gates promotion without halting all work.
fj: Security scanning slows us down, so we run it manually once in a while.

q: What's the difference between continuous integration, continuous delivery, and continuous deployment?
a: Continuous integration (CI) is merging every change to a shared branch frequently and proving it with automated build and tests on each push. Continuous delivery keeps that integrated code always in a deployable, release-ready state, but a human still clicks the button to ship to production. Continuous deployment removes that gate: every change passing the pipeline goes to production automatically with no manual approval. The two CDs share an acronym but differ only in whether the final prod release is manual or automatic.
fj: CI/CD is one thing, it just means automated deploys.
fs: CI = integrate + test on every push; delivery = always releasable with a manual gate; deployment = the same with the gate removed, so prod ships automatically.

q: What is the test pyramid and why does it shape how you run tests in CI?
a: The test pyramid layers a large base of fast, isolated unit tests, a middle band of integration tests, and a thin top of slow end-to-end tests. CI runs the cheap, broad base first so most regressions fail in seconds, and reserves the slow, brittle E2E layer for a small set of critical flows later in the pipeline. An inverted pyramid, mostly E2E, makes CI slow, flaky, and expensive, and hides where a failure actually is. The shape optimizes for fast, precise feedback at the lowest cost.
fj: I'll just write end-to-end tests since they cover everything.
fs: I put most coverage in fast unit tests and keep E2E thin, so CI fails in seconds and points precisely at the break instead of being slow and flaky.

q: Explain rolling deployment and how it differs from blue-green and canary.
a: A rolling deploy replaces instances of the old version with the new one in batches, draining and updating a few at a time until the whole fleet is upgraded, with no duplicate environment. Unlike blue-green's instant all-at-once cutover, traffic shifts gradually as each batch comes up healthy, and unlike canary it isn't a metric-gated small slice held for evaluation but a steady march to 100%. During the rollout both versions serve traffic, so the app and schema must tolerate mixed versions. Rollback means rolling the old version back batch by batch, which is slower than a blue-green flip.
fj: Rolling, blue-green, and canary all just mean a gradual release.
fs: Rolling updates the fleet in batches with no duplicate stack; blue-green is an instant full cutover, canary is a metric-gated slice. Rolling needs mixed-version tolerance and rolls back batch by batch.

q: Compare trunk-based development with GitFlow. Which suits CI/CD and why?
a: Trunk-based development has everyone commit to main (or very short-lived branches merged within a day), keeping the trunk always releasable and integrating constantly. GitFlow uses long-lived develop, release, and feature branches with scheduled merges, which suits infrequent versioned releases but causes painful big-bang merges and delays integration. CI/CD favors trunk-based because small frequent merges keep the pipeline green and the trunk deployable at all times. Long-lived branches in GitFlow defeat the 'continuous' in CI by letting code diverge for weeks before it's integrated and tested together.
fj: GitFlow is the modern best practice with its release and develop branches.
fs: Trunk-based keeps main always releasable with tiny frequent merges, which is what 'continuous integration' actually means. GitFlow's long-lived branches delay integration and cause big-bang merge pain.

q: What does 'pipeline as code' mean and why is it better than clicking through a CI UI?
a: Pipeline as code defines the build/test/deploy workflow in version-controlled files (a YAML workflow, Jenkinsfile, or similar) that live in the repo alongside the application. Because the pipeline is committed, every change is reviewed, diffed, and rolled back like any code, and the pipeline evolves atomically with the code it builds. UI-clicked job config is invisible to review, drifts silently, and can't be reproduced or audited. It also makes pipelines portable and lets you reuse shared, templated workflows across many repos.
fj: I configure the build steps in the CI dashboard and save them there.
fs: I keep the pipeline in versioned YAML in the repo so it's reviewed, diffable, and rolls back with the code. UI config drifts silently and can't be audited or reproduced.

q: How do you build a fast, safe CI pipeline for a dbt project?
a: Use Slim CI: instead of rebuilding every model on each PR, compare the PR's manifest to the last production manifest and run only state:modified models plus their downstream (state:modified+). The --defer flag points unchanged upstream models at the production environment so you don't rebuild them, and you build the changed models into a temporary PR-specific schema that's dropped after. Tests run against just the rebuilt slice, keeping CI fast and cheap. The one requirement is storing the production run's manifest.json (e.g. in S3) so CI can fetch it for the state comparison.
fj: On every PR I run dbt build to rebuild the whole project.
fs: Slim CI runs only state:modified+ models, defers unchanged upstreams to prod, and builds into a throwaway schema, so CI tests the changed slice in minutes instead of rebuilding everything.

q: How do feature flags let you decouple deployment from release, and why does that matter?
a: Deploying ships code to production; releasing exposes a feature to users. Wrapping new code in a feature flag lets you deploy it dark (off) and merge to trunk continuously, then flip it on later for a cohort or everyone, independent of the deploy. This enables dark launches, gradual percentage rollouts, and per-segment targeting without re-deploying. Critically, if a feature misbehaves you toggle the flag off in seconds instead of rolling back the whole deployment, slashing MTTR. The cost is flag lifecycle hygiene: stale flags become permanent branching debt and must be retired.
fj: A feature is released the moment its code is deployed to prod.
fs: A flag decouples deploy from release: I ship code dark on trunk, then flip it on per cohort, and kill a bad feature with a toggle instead of a full rollback, cutting MTTR. I budget for flag cleanup.

q: What makes a build reproducible, and why do hermetic builds matter for CI?
a: A reproducible build produces a bit-for-bit identical artifact from the same source every time, which requires removing nondeterminism: pinned dependency versions via a lockfile, pinned base images by digest, fixed toolchain versions, and stripped timestamps and embedded build paths. A hermetic build goes further by running in an isolated environment with no network access and all inputs explicitly declared, so it can't silently pull a different dependency or leak host state. This makes builds verifiable and cacheable and is a prerequisite for higher supply-chain assurance. Without it, two builds of the same commit can diverge, breaking the 'tested equals shipped' guarantee.
fj: As long as it builds green, reproducibility doesn't really matter.
fs: I pin deps by lockfile, base images by digest, and the toolchain, and run hermetically with no network, so the same commit yields a bit-identical artifact. That's what makes 'tested equals shipped' verifiable.

q: What is SLSA, and how do signed build provenance and artifact signing defend the supply chain?
a: SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) is a framework of graded levels for build integrity: Level 1 requires automatically generated provenance, Level 2 adds a hosted build platform that signs it, and Level 3 adds hardened, isolated builds with non-forgeable provenance. Provenance is a signed, machine-readable record of how an artifact was built, what source and inputs went in, and on which builder, so a consumer can verify the artifact wasn't swapped or built from tampered inputs. Tools like Sigstore/cosign sign artifacts and attestations, often keylessly via OIDC identity, and GitHub's artifact attestations get many builds to SLSA L2 quickly. At deploy time you verify the signature and provenance before promoting, rejecting anything unsigned or untrusted.
fj: Pushing the image to a private registry is enough supply-chain security.
fs: SLSA grades build integrity; I emit signed provenance (cosign, keyless via OIDC) attesting source and builder, and verify it at deploy so a tampered or swapped artifact is rejected before promotion.
